The Collection Through Time Hierarchy Trending Depth Geography Languages
The Collection

One third of every term describes a physical object.

The AAT organizes human material culture into 12 facets. But they're far from equal — Furnishings & Equipment alone holds 36.5% of all terms. From dental instruments to dough troughs, the thesaurus maps the objects civilizations have built, worn, and wielded.

16,157 terms for furnishings, tools, and equipment
Through Time

The 19th century casts the longest shadow.

Every scope note was mined for century references. The Industrial Revolution didn't just transform society — it generated an avalanche of vocabulary. 562 mentions of the 19th century, more than double any other. Styles & Periods is the most time-anchored facet. Color is timeless.

The Hierarchy

Click inward. This is how knowledge is organized.

Each ring peels back a layer of the taxonomy. Notice the "temporary alphabetical list" entries — terms awaiting classification in a living, curated system. The largest node is metalworking equipment with 316 children.

Trending Terms

When was each word at its peak?

Cross-referencing preferred terms with century mentions reveals temporal fingerprints. "Styles" surges in the 19th century as Revival movements exploded. "Paper" appears from the 15th century — the printing press rippling through vocabulary. Hover to trace each word's arc through time.

Depth

Some branches run eleven levels deep.

Styles & Periods spreads widest — from "Renaissance" near the root to nested pottery sub-styles. The deepest path?

Eukaryota → Animalia → Arthropoda → Hexapoda → Insecta → Lepidoptera → Papilionoidea → Nymphalidae → Morphinae → Morphini → Morpho → Morpho menelaus

A blue butterfly, eleven levels from the root.

Geography

Asia and Europe, neck and neck.

Asian references slightly edge out European (2,278 vs 2,264) in a Getty-originated thesaurus. European mentions peak during the colonial era. Asian references are strongest during the Tang and Song dynasties, then again in the 17th–19th centuries. Watch the regions shift as you scan across time.

Languages

334,694 names in scripts spanning the globe.

Every term averages 7.6 multilingual variants. Latin script dominates at 88.5%, but the CJK presence is striking — 11.3% of all variants are Chinese, Japanese, or Korean. Chinese architectural terms receive the richest scope notes in the entire dataset.

53 variants for "scrolled pediments" — the most translated term
